# Abstract {.unnumbered}

Aotearoa New Zealand has a rich history of cultural practices, including the harvest and trade of native freshwater crayfish (*Paranephrops planifrons*, kōura) by Māori communities. Over the past two decades, kōura populations have declined by up to 96% in Lake Rotoiti (North Island, New Zealand), making traditional harvesting unviable. This decline is likely driven by multiple stressors, including impaired water quality, intensified lake stratification, and the spread of invasive fish and aquatic weeds. Since 2016, the non-native brown bullhead catfish (*Ameiurus nebulosus*) has become established in the lake and preys on kōura. These threats indicate that restoring habitat is necessary for kōura population recovery.

To investigate the mechanisms behind kōura decline and test potential restoration tools, four studes were conducted: a behavioural experiment examining refuge use in response to native and novel predators, a multi-lake field survey of shoreline habitat associations, a mesocosm study teting habitat structure configurations and materials, and a field deployment of stone pile habitats in Lake Rotoiti.

The results showed that kōura reacted with more refuge use to the novel catfish than when exposed to native eels, complex shoreline habitats consisting of rocks and riparian vegetation were best predictors of kōura presence. When hole sizes were kept similar kōura had no preference between wood or stone material but when stability differed more stable structures are preferred also configurations that provided more complex refugia for individuals of different sizes is best suited for kōura recovery. Stone piles deployed in Lake Rotoiti attrected kōura at densities more comparable to natural rocky substrates than bare control sites, though differences over the 18-month monitoring period we not yet statistically significant. Extended monitoring is expected to reveal stronger population-level responses as kōura progresivly colonise the structures. 

Together, these findings suggest that stone pile deployment is a pormosing restoration tool that could support koura population recovery in degraded lake envioroments and in time, help restore both the ecological function and the costomary harvest practices that koura populations once sustained.
